title: Split main.go uname/machineID/systemUUID into main_linux.go and main_windows.go
description:
Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §5 (per-package map row for main.go) and §6 M1 (Node-level metrics on Windows — main.go must be cross-buildable before any other M1 work can ship).

WHAT TO DO
The current main.go contains three Linux-only helpers and one Linux-only check that prevent the program from compiling under GOOS=windows:
- uname()              — reads /proc/1/ns/uts and calls golang.org/x/sys/unix.Setns
- machineID()          — reads /etc/machine-id and similar Linux paths
- systemUUID()         — reads /sys/devices/virtual/dmi/id/product_uuid
- kernel-version gate  — exits if the running kernel is older than 4.16

Move all four into a new file main_linux.go. Keep the exported names and signatures byte-identical so the rest of main.go compiles unchanged. Then create main_windows.go with Windows-native implementations:
- uname():       hostname from os.Hostname(); OS version from golang.org/x/sys/windows.RtlGetVersion
- machineID():   read MachineGuid from HKLM SOFTWARE Microsoft Cryptography (REG_SZ) via golang.org/x/sys/windows/registry
- systemUUID():  read Win32_ComputerSystemProduct.UUID via WMI (e.g., github.com/yusufpapurcu/wmi or a similar package)
- kernel-version gate: no-op on Windows — return immediately, do not exit

WHY
main.go uses the return values of these helpers as Prometheus label values (machine_id, system_uuid) and as user-visible log lines. Everything in main.go past the helpers is platform-agnostic orchestration (HTTP server, signal handling, registry wiring) — only the four helpers need splitting. The build-tag discipline rule forbids runtime GOOS checks, so this split is compile-time only via the _linux.go and _windows.go suffix convention.

HOW TO VERIFY
- GOOS=linux   go build ./... still succeeds.
- GOOS=windows go build ./... now succeeds (it does not before this bead — it fails because uname uses unix.Setns which does not exist on Windows).
- On Linux, before vs. after this change, the binary's "hostname:", "kernel version:", and "machine-id:" startup log lines emit identical values for the same host. Run the binary briefly under each version and diff the startup output.
- On a Windows host (or in a Windows VM), the binary starts, logs a non-empty hostname and machine-id, and serves /metrics on the configured port.

EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS
- DO NOT modify the bodies of the four Linux helpers. The Linux-side diff for this bead is move-only — file rename to main_linux.go plus any import adjustments that fall out of the move. AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline" rule 1 forbids body changes to Linux source.
- machineID() on Windows MUST return a real value. main.go wraps it into a Prometheus label, and an empty string would corrupt metric series across restarts (a registry would treat empty-label-machine-A and empty-label-machine-B as the same series). Reading MachineGuid is a small amount of code and should be done as part of this bead, not deferred.
- systemUUID() via WMI is the most code-heavy of the three. If full WMI integration is out of scope for this bead's session, returning an empty string is acceptable as a temporary stub — but you MUST file a discovered-from bead capturing the gap and reference it in this bead's --notes before closing. systemUUID is used as a Prometheus label too, but unlike machineID, the existing Linux code returns the empty string when reading fails, so an empty Windows return value is consistent with Linux behavior.
- No runtime.GOOS == "windows" branching anywhere in main.go or anywhere else. Only build-tag separation via file suffix. AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline" rule 5.
- main_windows.go must import only Windows-portable packages. golang.org/x/sys/windows is fine; golang.org/x/sys/unix is forbidden. The Go toolchain will let you import unix in a _windows.go file (it'll just fail to link on Windows), so the build will catch it, but be explicit in the imports.

PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY
- "Build-tag discipline": the cardinal rule of this branch — Linux code stays unchanged; Windows logic lives in _windows.go sibling files (or parallel packages for ebpftracer). Full statement in AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline".
- "M1": the second milestone in plans/windows-port-plan.md §6, which covers node-level metrics on Windows. main.go must be cross-buildable before any other M1 work can ship.
- "Prometheus label": a string-valued tag attached to a metric (e.g., machine_id="abc123"). Stability of label values across restarts is essential — empty or unstable label values cause series fragmentation in the metrics backend.
acceptance_criteria: CRIT-1 (GOOS=windows go build ./... succeeds from a clean checkout) AND CRIT-3 (the Linux-side diff is move-only — no functional change to the bodies of uname, machineID, systemUUID, or the kernel-version gate). machineID() on Windows returns a non-empty value read from the MachineGuid registry key. systemUUID() may return an empty string as a temporary stub if WMI integration is deferred — in that case a discovered-from bead must be filed.

title: Add build-linux, build-windows, and crossbuild-check targets to Makefile
description:
Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 M0 third bullet (Makefile cross-build targets) and §9 CRIT-1 (which references make crossbuild-check as the enforcement mechanism for cross-build acceptance).

WHAT TO DO
Extend the existing Makefile (currently 30 lines, located at the repo root) to add three new targets that drive the cross-build verification used throughout the Windows port. Append the following targets AFTER the existing go-test target; do not modify any existing targets.

The three new targets:

    .PHONY: build-linux
    build-linux:
    <TAB>GOOS=linux go build ./...

    .PHONY: build-windows
    build-windows:
    <TAB>GOOS=windows go build ./...

    .PHONY: crossbuild-check
    crossbuild-check: build-linux build-windows
    <TAB>@echo "Both Linux and Windows builds succeeded."

Replace <TAB> with an ACTUAL TAB CHARACTER. Make requires hard tabs at the start of every recipe line. If you indent with spaces, make will print "missing separator" and fail. Use cat -A Makefile to confirm: recipe lines should start with ^I (the visible representation of a tab) in cat -A output.

WHY
AGENTS.md section "Sanity check before committing" mandates that every commit on this branch compiles under both GOOS=linux and GOOS=windows. Without a Makefile target, every developer (and CI later) has to type the two go build commands by hand, which is error-prone and slow to reference in code review and PR templates. plans/windows-port-plan.md §9 CRIT-1 names make crossbuild-check as the testable enforcement mechanism for the cross-build acceptance criterion — meaning the plan literally cannot be marked complete until this target exists and exits 0.

HOW TO VERIFY
- make build-linux exits 0 (it should, even before the rename and stub beads land, since the Linux build is already working).
- make build-windows exits 0 AFTER the rename, stub, and main.go-split beads have all landed. Before they land, this target will fail — that is expected and not this bead's responsibility to fix.
- make crossbuild-check exits 0 (after all three predecessor beads land) and prints the final line: Both Linux and Windows builds succeeded.
- The existing make lint and make test targets behave identically to before this bead. Run them both and confirm no behavior change.
- Temporarily break the Windows build (e.g., delete a _windows.go stub) and confirm make build-windows fails clearly. Restore the stub before committing.

EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS
- Tabs vs spaces: Makefile recipes MUST start with a tab character. Editors that auto-convert tabs to spaces (VS Code default in some configs, some vim setups) will break the targets silently. Verify with cat -A Makefile before committing.
- Do NOT make the new targets depend on lint or test. They build only. Mixing in lint or test would slow the cross-build check and would make it fail for unrelated reasons (e.g., a transient test flake). A separate target like make ci could chain build + lint + test in the future, but that is out of scope here.
- Do NOT add crossbuild-check as a dependency of the existing make all target. The default behavior should not silently invoke the Windows build for developers who do not have GOOS=windows tooling configured (some CGO-using packages may need extra setup).
- The crossbuild-check target lists build-linux and build-windows as ordered prerequisites; make will run them sequentially. Do not parallelize with -j N — they read and write the same module cache and may race.
- Cross-compiling Go itself does not require a Windows toolchain (Go ships its own cross-compilation support). However, CGO is enabled by default for some dependencies. If make build-windows fails with cgo errors after the rename and stub beads have landed, the failure indicates a Linux-coupled CGO callsite that the rename bead missed — file a discovered-from bead against the rename bead and do NOT paper over by force-disabling CGO globally. CGO_ENABLED=0 as a workaround should only be used as a last resort and with explicit user approval.

PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY
- "Cross-build": building the same Go source tree under two different GOOS environment variables (GOOS=linux and GOOS=windows) from the same checkout. Both must succeed for any commit on this branch to be valid.
- "crossbuild-check": the Makefile target this bead adds. plans/windows-port-plan.md §9 CRIT-1 names this target as the testable verification for the cross-build acceptance criterion.
- "M0": the first milestone in plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 — scaffolding only. This bead is one of five M0 deliverables.
acceptance_criteria: After the rename, stub, and main.go-split beads land, make crossbuild-check exits 0 from a clean checkout and prints the success message. make build-linux and make build-windows each invoke the correct GOOS-prefixed go build. The existing make lint and make test targets are unchanged in behavior. Tabs (not spaces) are used for recipe indentation.

title: Add stub _windows.go siblings to satisfy the API surface for GOOS=windows go build
description:
Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §5 (per-package map — most "Build-tag sibling" rows) and §6 M0 second bullet.

WHAT TO DO
For every Linux-coupled file that the rename bead gated to GOOS=linux only, add a sibling _windows.go file in the same package that satisfies the same exported Go API. The stubs do NOT need to actually function at runtime — they just need to make GOOS=windows go build ./... succeed by providing definitions for every exported symbol that platform-agnostic callers reference.

Procedure:
  1. After the rename bead has landed, run: GOOS=windows go build ./...
  2. For each compile error about an undefined symbol (function, type, variable, constant), find the Linux file that exports it.
  3. Create or extend the corresponding _windows.go file in the same package (e.g., the Linux-gated containers/registry.go gets a containers/registry_windows.go sibling).
  4. Add stubs for each missing exported symbol using these patterns:
     - Function returning an error only: return errors.New of a "not implemented on windows" string.
     - Function returning a value plus error: return zero value plus the same not-implemented error.
     - Function returning a prometheus.Collector: return a no-op collector. Define one in the package's _windows.go if needed — a no-op Collector implements Describe and Collect methods that do nothing.
     - Constructor that main.go would Exit on if it returned an error: return a real but inert value (e.g., an empty struct pointer) and nil error. Most can wait for M1+ to get real implementations.
     - Type: define the same struct or interface shape with empty fields; if a method is required by callers, give it a body that returns zero or an error.
  5. Add a //go:build windows line at the top of every _windows.go file even though the suffix already implies it. Explicit tags help grep and editor tooling.
  6. Re-run GOOS=windows go build ./... until it succeeds.
  7. Verify GOOS=linux go build ./... still succeeds — _windows.go files must be excluded from the Linux build automatically via their suffix.

What does NOT belong in this bead:
  - main.go (handled by the main.go-split sibling bead, which writes REAL Windows implementations — not stubs).
  - The ebpftracer/ package: it stays Linux-only with no Windows sibling. The Windows analogue is in ebpfwin/ and etwtracer/, which are M3 work. If a non-ebpftracer file imports ebpftracer/ symbols and that breaks the Windows build after stubs land here, gate the IMPORTING file as Linux-only (i.e., that caller becomes Linux-only too) and revisit in M3.

WHY
After the rename bead, the Windows build fails because Linux-only symbols (e.g., containers.NewRegistry, node.NewCollector) are excluded from the Windows build but their platform-agnostic callers (e.g., main.go after its own split lands) still reference them. Stubs satisfy the linker without committing to any actual Windows runtime behavior. This is the smallest possible change that unblocks GOOS=windows go build ./... and is purely additive — no Linux file is touched.

HOW TO VERIFY
- GOOS=windows go build ./... exits 0.
- GOOS=linux go build ./... still exits 0 (no regression).
- For every Linux-coupled file gated by the rename bead, the package now has a sibling _windows.go (or shares one with related files in the same package). The two files together define the same set of exported symbols. Spot-check with: for each package, grep for exported function and type declarations in both files and confirm the Windows set is a superset of (or equal to) what platform-agnostic callers actually use.
- No stub does real Windows work. Every function body returns a zero value, an error, a no-op type, or is empty. M1+ beads replace stubs with real implementations once the rest of the scaffolding is in place.

EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS
- Only EXPORTED symbols (capitalized names) need Windows stubs. Unexported helpers are only referenced from within their own file and do not need to exist on Windows.
- If a Linux file is referenced only from another Linux file (and both are gated together), no Windows stub is needed for either. Let the Windows compiler tell you what is actually required — do not pre-empt by stubbing things that nobody references.
- Stubs returning errors at runtime are FINE for M0 because nothing executes the Windows binary yet. By the end of M1, every stub that runs during normal startup must be replaced with a real implementation; that is M1+ scope.
- Do NOT add tests for stubs in this bead. Tests come with the real implementations in M1+. Adding _windows_test.go files that assert returns-not-implemented behavior is wasted work — those tests will be deleted as soon as the real impl lands.
- Be careful with package-level variables. If a Linux file initializes a package-level var via a Linux-only call, the Windows stub file must also initialize the same var (or the package will fail to load on Windows). The simplest pattern: declare the var as a nil or zero-value default in a portable file in the same package.

PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY
- "Stub": a placeholder Windows implementation that compiles but does not function correctly at runtime. Stubs unblock the build; real implementations come later in M1+.
- "Sibling file": a _windows.go file in the same package as a _linux.go file, exposing the same exported Go API. This bead creates the sibling stubs; the rename bead created the gated Linux files.
- "M0 / M1 / M3": milestones in plans/windows-port-plan.md §6. M0 is scaffolding (this bead). M1 starts replacing stubs with real implementations for node-level metrics. M3 introduces parallel tracing packages (ebpfwin/, etwtracer/).
acceptance_criteria: CRIT-1 (GOOS=windows go build ./... succeeds after this bead) AND CRIT-3 (no functional changes to existing Linux source — every Windows file added is a new _windows.go sibling whose function bodies return errors, zero values, or no-op collectors). Verified by running both cross-builds from a clean checkout after the rename bead has landed.

title: Gate existing Linux-coupled Go files with //go:build linux or _linux.go suffix
description:
Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §5 (per-package map — most rows are tagged "Build-tag sibling" or "Linux-only" and need gating) and §6 M0 first bullet.

WHAT TO DO
Identify every existing Go source file that depends on Linux-only APIs and gate it so it is only compiled when GOOS=linux. A file is "Linux-coupled" if it imports any of: golang.org/x/sys/unix, github.com/cilium/ebpf/*, github.com/vishvananda/netlink, github.com/vishvananda/netns, github.com/coreos/go-systemd/v22/*, github.com/godbus/dbus/v5, github.com/mdlayher/taskstats, github.com/florianl/go-conntrack, github.com/containerd/cgroups, or the Linux-only parts of github.com/containerd/containerd. A file is also Linux-coupled if it reads /proc, /sys, /etc/machine-id, /var/log/journal, or similar Linux-only filesystem paths.

For each such file, apply ONE of these mechanical changes (prefer rename when the file is 100% Linux-only):
  - Rename the file to use the _linux.go suffix (e.g., containers/cilium.go becomes containers/cilium_linux.go). The Go toolchain treats this filename suffix as a build constraint implicitly.
  - Add a //go:build linux header on the very first line of the file, followed by a blank line, followed by the package declaration. Use this form when renaming would be confusing.

Files already correctly gated (e.g., cgroup/cgroup_linux.go) do NOT need to be touched.

DO NOT touch main.go in this bead — main.go is handled by the main.go-split sibling bead. If you encounter Windows-build errors mentioning main.go after this bead's renames, leave them; the main.go bead will fix them.

The work is iterative and driven by build failures:
  1. From a clean checkout, run: GOOS=windows go build ./...
  2. For each error about an undefined symbol, unresolved import, or non-existent package on Windows, identify the source file that owns the offending import or symbol.
  3. Apply the rename or the //go:build linux header to that file.
  4. Re-run step 1. Continue until the only remaining errors are about missing exported symbols that need a _windows.go stub (covered by the stub bead, not this one).
  5. Confirm GOOS=linux go build ./... still succeeds after every rename and header addition.

Expected packages requiring action (NON-EXHAUSTIVE — drive from compile errors, not from this list): cgroup/, containers/, ebpftracer/, logs/, node/, pinger/, proc/, profiling/, and parts of common/. The complete set is whatever the Windows compiler complains about.

WHY
The Linux-coupled imports above prevent GOOS=windows from compiling at all. Gating those files makes the Linux build identical (it still includes them) but excludes them from the Windows build, where their imports cannot resolve. This is the foundation for the parallel-package and sibling-file patterns that the rest of the Windows port relies on. AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline" rule 1 mandates that this is the only category of change permitted to Linux source files.

HOW TO VERIFY
- GOOS=linux go build ./... succeeds (it was succeeding before; it must still succeed).
- GOOS=windows go build ./... fails ONLY with errors about missing exported symbols that need _windows.go stubs. No errors about undefined imports, non-existent packages on Windows, or undefined syscalls.
- git diff --stat shows only file renames and at most one //go:build linux header line added per affected file. No body changes. No reordering of existing code. No reformatting. Inspect every changed file in the PR review and confirm.
- A grep for any Linux-only import (e.g., grep -rn golang.org/x/sys/unix in non-_linux.go files outside this bead's scope) returns nothing for files that the Windows build still tries to compile.

EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS
- The //go:build linux line must be on the FIRST line of the file, with a blank line between it and the package declaration. The older space-prefixed comment syntax is also accepted by Go but the //go:build form is required since Go 1.17, and this repo is on Go 1.24.
- Some files import portable packages like github.com/prometheus/client_golang/prometheus but ALSO have Linux-only code via os.Open of /proc paths or syscall calls. These files are still Linux-coupled and must be gated. The rule of thumb: if the file requires a Linux runtime to function, it is Linux-coupled regardless of how portable its imports look.
- Test files (*_test.go) follow the same rule. A Linux-only test must use the _linux_test.go suffix (e.g., foo_linux_test.go) or a //go:build linux header.
- If a file would be portable with trivial Go changes (e.g., a one-line conditional-equivalent), do NOT make those portable changes in this bead. File a discovered-from bead noting the opportunity and leave the file Linux-only for now. Cleanliness of this M0 diff matters more than perfect Windows coverage; later beads can revisit.
- The ebpftracer/ package is Linux-only by design (see AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline" rule 3). Every file in that package should end up gated; the Windows tracing analogue lives in separate parallel packages ebpfwin/ and etwtracer/ that are M3 work, not M0.
- After this bead, callers of Linux-only packages from platform-agnostic code (e.g., main.go calling containers.NewRegistry) will fail to compile on Windows. That is EXPECTED — the stub bead adds _windows.go stubs that satisfy the API surface.

PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY
- "Linux-coupled": a file whose code depends on Linux-specific runtime behavior (kernel APIs, /proc, eBPF, netlink, etc.), even if some of its imports are portable.
- "Build-tag discipline": the cardinal rule of this branch — Linux code stays byte-identical wherever possible; Windows logic lives in _windows.go sibling files or parallel packages. Full statement in AGENTS.md section "Build-tag discipline".
- "Sibling file": a _windows.go file in the same package as a _linux.go file. Sibling stubs are added by the next bead in M0, not this one.
- "Parallel package": ebpftracer/'s Windows analogue lives in ebpfwin/ and etwtracer/, not as a _windows.go file inside ebpftracer/. That is M3 scope, not M0.
acceptance_criteria: CRIT-3 (no functional changes to Linux source — only file renames or single //go:build linux header lines permitted). Verified by code review of the diff: every changed file is either renamed to _linux.go suffix or has exactly one //go:build linux header line added; no function bodies are modified. After this bead, GOOS=linux go build ./... still succeeds; GOOS=windows go build ./... fails only with errors about missing exported symbols that the stub bead will provide.

title: M0: Build-tag scaffolding for Windows port
description:
Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 M0 (Scaffolding milestone).

WHAT TO DO
This is the milestone-tracking epic for M0. It does not require code changes itself — its child beads do the work. Close this epic only when:
  1. All five M0 child beads (build-tag headers, _windows.go stubs, Makefile cross-build targets, main.go split, README/CONTRIBUTING update) have closed.
  2. The exit gate is demonstrably passed: make crossbuild-check exits 0 from a clean checkout, and make test on a Linux host is green.

When closing, run:
    bd close <this-epic-id> --reason "M0 exit gate passed: both cross-builds green, Linux tests unchanged."

WHY
M0 isolates the mechanical build-tag plumbing from any Windows feature work. Until both cross-builds succeed from a clean checkout, no later Windows feature bead can land cleanly — every later bead would have to fight build errors on Windows that are not its responsibility to fix. M0 makes M1–M5 buildable on Windows by construction; the actual Windows runtime behavior is empty (stub) at M0 exit.

HOW TO VERIFY
- make crossbuild-check exits 0 from a clean checkout. (This target is added by one of the child beads; until then verify manually with: GOOS=linux go build ./... && GOOS=windows go build ./...)
- make test on a Linux host: PASS, with no test count or duration regression beyond noise.
- git log on Linux source files (excluding renames) shows zero functional changes attributable to M0 beads. Permitted Linux-side changes: //go:build linux header line additions, file renames to _linux.go suffix, and the move-only main.go split into main_linux.go.

EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS
- Do NOT close this epic when child beads close. Close it ONLY when the exit gate has been independently verified. Per AGENTS.md §"Workflow: plans → beads → code → plan-complete" rule 4, plan/milestone closure is gated by acceptance criteria, not by child-bead closure.
- If a child bead discovers in-scope work it can't finish (e.g., the WMI integration for systemUUID() is more involved than expected), the child closes by filing a follow-up bead via discovered-from linkage. The follow-up does NOT belong to M0 unless it blocks the exit gate. Default placement: M1 backlog or its own milestone.
- M0 deliberately does not change any user-visible runtime behavior on Linux. If a Linux behavior shifts during M0, something has gone wrong — investigate before merging.

PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY
- "Cross-build" — building the same Go source tree under different GOOS environment variables: GOOS=linux go build ./... and GOOS=windows go build ./.... Both must succeed for this branch to be valid.
- "Build-tag discipline" — the cardinal rule of this branch: Linux code stays byte-identical wherever possible; Windows logic lives in _windows.go sibling files or parallel packages. Full rules in AGENTS.md §"Build-tag discipline".
- "Exit gate" — the milestone-specific verification step in plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 that must pass before the milestone is marked complete. For M0 it's "make crossbuild-check exits 0 and make test on Linux is unchanged".
acceptance_criteria: CRIT-1 (GOOS=linux go build ./... AND GOOS=windows go build ./... both succeed from a clean checkout) AND CRIT-2 (existing Linux test suite still passes via make test, no regressions) AND CRIT-3 (no functional changes to any Linux source file — only build-tag header additions or _linux.go renames). All five child beads MUST be closed before this epic closes.

title: Add Windows-port-in-progress note to README.md
description: Plan: plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 M0 fourth bullet (Update CONTRIBUTING.md and README.md platform claims, note Windows is in-progress) and §9 CRIT-6 (final state must list Windows as supported — but the final state is NOT reached in M0). WHAT TO DO Add a "Windows port (in progress)" note to README.md that signals to anyone cloning this fork that Windows support is being worked on, and points them to AGENTS.md and plans/windows-port-plan.md for status. CONTRIBUTING.md already received a one-line fork-note pointing to AGENTS.md in a prior session — DO NOT modify CONTRIBUTING.md in this bead. In README.md, insert the following block on its own paragraph AFTER the existing image line (currently README.md line 10, the tag for the eBPF blog illustration) and BEFORE the "## Features" section header: > **Windows port (in progress):** This fork of coroot-node-agent is being extended with Windows support on the `windows-port` branch. The descriptions below reflect the Linux build, which is unchanged. Windows feature parity is being delivered in milestones — see [`AGENTS.md`](AGENTS.md) for the agent workflow and [`plans/windows-port-plan.md`](plans/windows-port-plan.md) for the design and current status. That is the ONLY change to README.md. Do NOT edit the existing text. Do NOT update the Features section, the Installation section, the Metrics section, or any eBPF reference. Those updates are M5 scope (plans/windows-port-plan.md §6 M5 documents the final user-visible Windows support story). WHY plans/windows-port-plan.md §9 CRIT-6 requires the FINAL state of README.md and CONTRIBUTING.md to list Windows as a fully supported platform. M0 does not reach that final state — it reaches only "Windows build compiles, runtime is stubbed". The in-progress note is the M0-appropriate signal: it tells a fresh visitor that Windows is being worked on, without falsely claiming current support and without overwriting accurate Linux-build descriptions. HOW TO VERIFY - Render README.md (e.g., open in GitHub UI or with a local Markdown previewer). The blockquote should appear as a callout above the Features section. - The string "windows-port" or "Windows port" appears EXACTLY once in README.md — in the inserted block. This is the canonical M0 mention. - The Linux feature descriptions in the README are unchanged byte-for-byte except for the inserted block (verify with git diff README.md). - CONTRIBUTING.md is unchanged in this bead (verify with git diff CONTRIBUTING.md showing no changes). EDGE CASES AND PITFALLS - The links to AGENTS.md and plans/windows-port-plan.md are RELATIVE paths (no leading https URL). Relative paths work in the GitHub UI for any fork and survive repo renames. Do NOT use absolute URLs pointing at NVShawn/coroot-node-agent — if the fork is renamed or upstreamed, absolute URLs would rot. - Do NOT adjust the README's eBPF claims yet. The eBPF text is accurate for the Linux build, which is what the README is describing. M5 will rewrite the feature matrix and platform sections; that is not this bead's job. - The blockquote uses standard GitHub-flavored Markdown (a > prefix). Do NOT use GitHub's named-alert syntax (the bracket-bang-LABEL forms like NOTE / WARNING) here — those render only on github.com and not in other Markdown viewers. The plain blockquote with a bold prefix is portable. - Do NOT touch the existing badges block at the top of README.md (Go Report Card, License). They are accurate as-is. PROJECT-SPECIFIC TERMINOLOGY - "Fork-note": a one-line statement at the top of CONTRIBUTING.md (already added in a prior session) that points readers to AGENTS.md for fork-internal rules. - "M0 / M5": milestones in plans/windows-port-plan.md §6. M0 is scaffolding (this bead is M0). M5 is packaging and the final documentation-parity step, where the README's feature matrix gets rewritten. - "windows-port branch": the branch this fork is using for the port. The branch name is referenced in the README text so visitors know where to look.
acceptance_criteria: README.md contains a Windows-port-in-progress blockquote linking AGENTS.md and plans/windows-port-plan.md, inserted between the existing eBPF image line and the ## Features section. CONTRIBUTING.md is unchanged in this bead (a fork-note pointer to AGENTS.md was already added in a prior session). The CRIT-6 final state (Windows listed as fully supported) is NOT yet reached — this bead provides M0-appropriate in-progress signaling only.
